content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


September 8, 2009




Eileen M. Teichert
City Attorney, City of Sacramento
P.O. Box 1948
Sacramento, CA. 95812

Re:     Your Request for Informal Assistance
        Our File No. I-09-207

Dear Ms. Teichert:

        This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Sacramento City
Council and the Mayor, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that
may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to
prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political
Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)²

        Because your request partly concerns conduct that has already taken place and partly
concerns future actions of indefinite scope, we are providing informal assistance based on the
position you pose rather than formal advice concerning a specific decision before the City Council.
(Section 83114 and Regulation 18329(c)(4)(A) and (F).) Informal assistance does not provide

_________________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² We note that, on September 1, 2009, we received a separate request for advice on the same issues from
Thomas Hiltachk, attorney for City of Sacramento Mayor Kevin Johnson. We are issuing a separate advice letter to
Mr. Hiltachk (see Hiltachk Advice Letter, No. A-09-213) on the issue of whether Mayor Johnson, as matters
currently stand, can participate in City Council decisions on the issues discussed below. We view the essential facts
provided in Mr. Hiltachk's request as supplemental to those provided in your request and, therefore, our advice
herein is based on the facts in both your advice request and Mr. Hiltachk's.

Our File No. I-09-207
Page 2

the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice (see Section
83114 and Regulation 18329(c)(3)). Nevertheless we offer this assistance as guidance for your
city councilmembers, including the Mayor, on potential future decisions involving the issues
discussed below.

Finally, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of conflict-of-interest laws outside the Act
such as common law conflict of interest.

QUESTION

Does the Mayor have a conflict of interest under the Act if the Mayor participates in
future decisions before the City Council relating to placing a measure on the ballot that corrects
perceived flaws in another measure already on the same ballot when the ballot measure
committee that qualified the first measure has a current outstanding loan from the Mayor in
excess of $500?

CONCLUSION

Under the facts presented, the Mayor would not have a conflict of interest because it is
not reasonably foreseeable that city council decisions related to placing the second measure on
the ballot will have a material financial effect on the ballot measure committee or the Mayor.

FACTS

Sacramento's current charter established a Council-Manager form of government, with a
nine member City Council composed of the Mayor and eight councilmembers. The Mayor is
campaigning for an initiative measure that would amend Sacramento's charter, changing its form
of government to a "Mayor-Council" model. On or about June 4, 2009, the Mayor loaned
personal funds to Sacramentans for Accountable Government ("SAG"), a
recipient committee primarily formed to qualify and support the foregoing initiative measure,
known as "The Strong Mayor Initiative" and another measure to create an Office of the
Independent Budget Analyst to provide independent budget and fiscal information to the City
Council. In the spring 2009, "The Strong Mayor Initiative" qualified for the ballot, requiring the
City Council to place it before the voters on a future ballot. On August 6, 2009, the City Council
voted to put the measure to a vote on June 8, 2010.

On August 25, 2009, the City Council noticed consideration of matters relating to
possible flaws in the measure, and potential curative actions that the City Council might take,
described in a "Legal Issues & Options Report." Among these possible actions would be for the
City Council to place a complementary measure on the same ballot as "The Strong Mayor
Initiative" to correct flaws in that initiative. Prior to this meeting, however, a member of the
public and a councilmember asked whether the Mayor's economic interest in SAG might pose a

Our File No. 1-09-207
Page 3


conflict of interest disqualifying him from participation in votes that would have a reasonably
foreseeable, material financial effect on the SAG.

Based on your analysis, the conflict of interest issue is essentially grounded on the
following: (1) The Mayor is a creditor of SAG because of the outstanding loan of $25,000 owed
to the Mayor by the Committee; (2) The most recent campaign reports (as of June 30, 2009)
show SAG carrying debt of $149,977.90 and cash on hand of $20,993.54, or, essentially, net debt
of approximately $129,000; (3) If the City Council decides to not place a corrective measure on
the same ballot as "The Strong Mayor Initiative," you believe SAG is likely to attempt to do the
same by the initiative process, thereby incurring significant costs; (4) If SAG incurs these
additional expenses, this presumably would affect its ability to repay the $25,000 loan owed to
the Mayor. On this basis, you advised that the Mayor might have a conflict of interest but the
Mayor disagreed. The City Council, including Mayor Johnson, voted 9-0 to continue the matter
for two weeks so the City Attorney could obtain advice on the conflict issue from the
Commission.

Other pertinent information relating to SAG includes the following: SAG qualified as a
committee on December 12, 2008. The Mayor neither controls SAG nor is he a principal officer
of the committee. During the period from January 1, 2009 and June 30, 2009, SAG received
$91,724 in contributions and made $202,208.36 in expenditures. Thomas Hiltachk, attorney for
the Mayor and treasurer for SAG, indicates that neither he nor the Mayor believe there are legal
problems with "The Strong Mayor Initiative," that no corrective measure needs to be placed on
the ballot to correct perceived flaws in the proposal. He further indicates that neither he, as the
proponent of "the Strong Mayor Initiative," nor SAG, intend to draft or circulate a petition to
place their own corrective measure on the ballot in the event the City Council fails to do so.

On August 25, 2009, the Mayor forgave the loan to SAG, converting what had been a
loan to a contribution to SAG. Therefore, as of the date of this letter, the Mayor has no
economic interest in SAG under the Act. However, given the pending ballot measure campaign
on "The Strong Mayor Initiative" and, possibly, a corrective measure being placed on the ballot
to correct perceived flaws in "The Strong Mayor Initiative," it is possible additional loans could
be made by the Mayor to SAG. On this basis, the City Council, including the Mayor, is seeking
guidance on whether the Mayor may participate in future council decisions on this issue if, prior
to those decisions, he makes another loan to SAG. The decisions in question would include
choosing options set forth in the "Legal Issues and Options Report," designating
councilmembers to author official statements in support or opposition to the one or both of the
initiatives, or approving the City Attorney's analysis of legal issues raised in this process.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions are designed to ensure that public officials will
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise

Our File No. J-09-207
Page 4


using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the meaning
of the Act if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual
has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.

Step One: Is the Mayor a "public official?"

The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .." (Section 82048 .) The Mayor is a public
official within the meaning of the Act.³

Step Two: Is the Mayor making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

By voting on or otherwise participating in upcoming City Council decisions regarding
options outlined in the Legal Issues & Options Report or decisions such as designating
councilmembers to author statements supporting or opposing the initiative or approving the City
Attorney's legal analysis of issues raised in this process, the Mayor would be making or
participating in a governmental decision.


_____________
³ If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 files" include mayors and city
councilmembers) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved
in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation 18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record
of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on
the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding
personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

Our File No. 1-09-207
Page 5


Step Three: What are the economic interests of the Mayor?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• An economic interest in a source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• An economic interest in a source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Because you have not provided any facts concerning other possible economic interests of the Mayor, our analysis is limited to economic interests similar to those the Mayor may have had in, or on account of, his association with SAG by virtue of the loan he made to SAG.

We observe that on August 25, 2009, the Mayor forgave the $25,000 loan to SAG. Therefore, as set forth in the Hiltachk Advice Letter, No. A-09-213, the Mayor does not have a current economic interest in SAG and, absent making another loan of $500 or more or incurring some other type of economic interest in SAG, the Mayor is not precluded from participating in future City Council decisions of the type described above. However, we will continue our analysis of this issue to provide guidance for the Mayor and City Council in the event the Mayor makes a future loan to SAG of $500 or more in connection with the upcoming ballot measure election and these decisions remain before the City Counsel.

Our File No. 1-09-207
Page 6

The facts indicate the Mayor does not control SAG and is not a principal officer of the
committee and therefore has no potential liability for committee debts.⁴ Nevertheless, an official
may still have an economic interest in a committee when, for example, he or she has assumed
personal liability for the debts of the committee, receives gifts or income from it, or the effects of
governmental decisions on the committee impact the official's personal liabilities.

Here, it appears there are two economic interests potentially at issue for the Mayor: his
interest in SAG as a source of income to him and the Mayor's interest in his personal finances.

Source of Income. If SAG has not made payments of $500 or more to the Mayor on the
loan, it would not yet be an actual source of income to the Mayor for purposes of the Act's
conflict-of-interest provisions. (See Section 87103(c).) However, as stated above, Section
87103(c) provides that an individual or entity is a source of income to an official for purposes of
the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions if, among other things, the individual or entity promises
income of $500 or more to the official during the 12-month period before the official participates
in a governmental decision having a financial effect on the source of income. Regulation
18703.3(a) elaborates on this concept, stating: "A public official's income includes income
which has been promised to the public official but not yet received by him or her, if he or she has
a legally enforceable right to the promised income." Therefore, assuming the Mayor had an
enforceable right to repayment of the loan to SAG, SAG would remain a source of promised
income to the Mayor as long as the loan balance remained at $500 or more.

Personal Financial Effect. If SAG owed the Mayor on a loan, any governmental decision
having a reasonably foreseeable effect on repayment of the loan would have a personal financial
effect on the Mayor. As stated in Regulation 18703.5, a governmental decision will have a
personal financial effect "if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing."

On these two bases, the Mayor would have an economic interest in governmental
decisions affecting SAG.

Step 4. Will the Mayor's economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions
he will make, participate in making or influence as a public official?

Source of Income. A person, including a source of income, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent (1) initiates
the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal or similar request; or (2) is a named party
in, or is the subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation 18704.1.) Because SAG will not be
initiating any proceeding before the City Council regarding the corrective ballot measure, and is
neither a named party in, nor the subject of, the proceeding, SAG is not directly involved.
Therefore, SAG is indirectly involved in the decisions before the City Council. (See Regulation
18704(a).)

⁴ We do not in this letter offer an analysis or opinion of whether a candidate, committee treasurer or
committee principal officer is personally liable for the debts of the committee.

Our File No. 1-09-207
Page 7

Personal Financial Effect. However, as to the personal financial effect economic interest
of the Mayor, Regulation 18704.5 provides as follows: "A public official or his or her immediate
family are deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision which has any financial
effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." Therefore the
Mayor is deemed to be directly involved in the decisions before the City Council relating to the
corrective ballot measure.

Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)

Source of Income. As a recipient committee under the Act, SAG is considered a non-
profit entity for purposes of determining the materiality standard. According to the facts
presented, SAG was formed in December 2008 and raised $91,724 in contributions between
January 1 and June 30, 2009. Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(F) states that the financial effect of a
government decision is material for an indirectly involved non-profit entity whose gross annual
receipts are $100,000 or less, stating as follows:

"(F) For an entity whose gross annual receipts are $100,000 or less, the
effect of the decision will be any of the following:
(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's gross
annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.
(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses
or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount
of $2,500 or more.
(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the
entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more."

Thus, assuming SAG's total contributions received during 2009 remain at $100,000 or
less, Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(F) will control the materiality threshold. If, however, SAG's
contributions exceed $100,000 in 2009 but do not exceed $1,000,000, then Regulation
18705.3(b)(2)(E), will apply, which states as follows:

"(E) For an entity whose gross annual receipts are more than $100,000 but
less than or equal to $1,000,000 the effect of the decision will be any of
the following:
(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's gross
annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 or more.
(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses
or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount
of $12,500 or more.

Our File No. 1-09-207
Page 8

(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the
entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $50,000 or more."

Personal Financial Effect. As to the materiality threshold for the personal financial
effect of the City Council's decisions on the Mayor, Regulation 18705.5(a) provides, in pertinent
part, as follows:

"(a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's or his
or her immediate family's personal finances is material if it is at least $250
in any 12-month period."

Therefore, if the effect of the City Council's decisions on the Mayor's personal finances is $250
or more in any 12-month period, they will be material for purposes of this analysis.

Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decisions
on the Mayor's economic interest will meet that applicable materiality standard?

An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made
depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. (In re Thorner, supra.) A financial
effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an
effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

Thus, we have to determine whether it is substantially likely that actions of the City
Council affecting whether or not the corrective measure will be placed on the ballot will have
any of the following effects:

(1) Increasing or decreasing the amount in contributions made to SAG in a fiscal year, or
the value of SAG's assets or liabilities, by $10,000 or more (or by $50,000 or more if total
contributions to SAG in 2009 are more than $100,000 and up to $1,000,000).

(2) Causing SAG to incur or avoid additional expenses or reduce or eliminate existing
expenses in a fiscal year of $10,000 or more (or $50,000 or more if total contributions to SAG in
2009 are more than $100,000 and up to $1,000,000).

(3) Resulting in the personal expenses income, assets, or liabilities of the Mayor
increasing or decreasing by $250 or more in any 12-month period.

In assessing this issue, we think it is of great significance that Mr. Hiltachk, the Mayor's
attorney and treasurer for SAG, indicates that even if the City Council does not vote to place the
corrective measure on the ballot, neither he, as the proponent of "The Strong Mayor Initiative,"
nor SAG intend to take any action to draft or circulate a petition to place its own corrective

Our File No. 1-09-207
Page 9


measure on the ballot. In addition, Mr. Hiltachk states that he sees no legal problems with "The
Strong Mayor Initiative" and thus sees no reason to place any corrective measure on the ballot.

Based on these facts, it is reasonable to assume that SAG would not be involved at all in
issues relating to the corrective measure and any campaign contributions it raises or debts it
incurs will relate solely to the two measures it was primarily formed to support: "The Strong
Mayor Initiative" and the measure to create the City Office of the Independent Budget Analyst.
If this is the case, we fail to see any significant connection between the City Council's decisions
on placing the corrective measure on the ballot and a financial impact on SAG or on SAG's
ability to repay a loan to the Mayor. In addition, we think it would be highly speculative to
assert, and thus not substantially likely, that the existence of the corrective measure on the ballot
might affect, either positively or negatively, SAG's ability to raise contributions or otherwise
affect SAG's debts or liabilities. Accordingly, we do not think it is reasonably foreseeable that
the City Council's decisions affecting placement of a corrective measure on the ballot will have a
material financial effect on SAG or on the Mayor's personal finances.

Thus, we conclude that, based on the facts provided, the Mayor would not have a conflict
of interest because it is not reasonably foreseeable that council decisions related to placing the
corrective measure on the ballot will have a material financial effect on SAG or the Mayor.

Steps 7 & 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation" exceptions.

Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)), or if the official is
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). Since we have already
concluded that the Mayor does not have a conflict of interest in this instance, it is not necessary
to provide analysis of Steps 7 and 8.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he
or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703. l(b).)
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest
of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
An economic interest in a source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3.)
An economic interest in a source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420 or
more within l2 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);.Regulation 18703.4.)
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section
87 103 ; Regulation 1 8703.5.)
Because you have not provided any facts conceming other possible economic interests of
the Mayor, our analysis is limited to economic interests similar to those the Mayor may have had
in, or on account of, his association with SAG by virtue of the loan he made to SAG.
We observe that on August 25,2009, the Mayor forgave the $25,000loan to SAG.
Therefore, as set forth in the Hiltachl< Advice Letter, No. ,4'-09-213, the Mayor does not have a
current economic interest in sAG and, absent making another loan of $500 or more or incurring
some other type of economic interest in SAG, the Mayor is not precluded from participating in
future City Council decisions of the type described above. However, we will continue our
analysis of this issue to provide guidance for the Mayor and City Council in the event the Mayor
makes a future loan to SAG of $500 or more in connection with the upcoming ballot measure-
election and these decisions remain before the City Counsel.
a
o
a
a


Our Page 6
The facts indicate the Mayor does not control SAG and is not a principal officer of the
committee and therefore has no potential liability for committee debts.l Nevertheless, an official
may still have an economic interest in a committee when, for example, he or she has assumed
personal liability for the debts of the committee, receives gifts or income from it, or the effects of
governmental decisions on the committee impact the official's personal finances.
Here, it appears there.are two economic interests potentially at issue for the Mayor: his
interest in SAG as a source of income to him and the Mayor's interest in his personal finances.
Source of Income. If SAG has not made payments of $500 or more to the Mayor on the
loan, it would not yet be an actual source of income to the Mayor for purposes of the Act's
conflicrof-interest provisions. (See Section 87103(c).) However, as stated above, Section
87103(c) provides that an individual or entity is a source of income to an official for purposes of
the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions if, among other things, the individual or entity piomises
income of $500 or more to the official during the l2-month period before the official prrti"iprt.,
in a governmental decision having a financial effect on the source of income. Regulaiion
18703.3(a) elaborates on this concept, stating: "A public official's income includes income
which has been promised to the public official but not yet received by him or her, if he or she has
a legally enforceable right to the promised income." Therefore, assuming the Mayor had an
enforceable right to repayment of the loan to SAG, SAG would remain a source ol promised
income to the Mayor as long as the loan balance remained at $500 or more.
Personal Financial Effect. If SAG owed the Mayor on a loan, any governmental decision
having a reasonably foreseeable effect on repayment of the loan would have a personal financial
effect on the Mayor. As stated in Regulation 18703.5, a governmental decision will have a
personal financial effect "if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing."
On these two bases, the Mayor would have an economic interest in governmental
decisions affecting SAG.
Step 4. Will the Mayor's economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions
he will make, participate in making or influence as a pubtic official?
Source of Income. A person, including a source of income, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent (l) initiates
the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal or similar request; or (2) is a named party
in, or is the subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation 18704.1.) Because SAG will not be
initiating any proceeding before the City Council regarding the corrective ballot measure, and is
neither a named party in, nor the subject of the proceeding, SAG is not directly involved.
Therefore, SAG is indirectly involved in the decisions before the City Councii. (See Regulation
18704(a).)
'we do not in this letter ofter an analysis or opinion of whether a candidate, committee treasurer or
committee principal oftlcer is personally liable for the debts of the committee.


Our PageT
Personal Financial Effect. However, as to the personal financial effect economic interest
of the Mayor, Regulation L8704.5 provides as follows: "A public official or his or her immediate
family are deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision which has any financial
effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." Therefore the
Mayor is deemed to be directly involved in the decisions before the City Council relating to the
corrective ballot measure.
Step 5. What is the applicabte materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
goverrlmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)
Source of Income. As a recipient committee under the Act, SAG is considered a non-
profit entity for purposes of determining the materiality standard. According to the facts
presented, SAG was formed in December 2008 and raised $91,724 in contributions between
January 1 and June 30,2009. Regulation 18705.3(b)(2XF) states rhat the financial effect of a
government decision is material for an indirectly involved non-profit entity whose gross annual
receipts are $100,000 or less, stating as follows:
"(F) For an entity whose gross annual receipts are $100,000 or less, the
effect of the decision will be any of the following:
(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's gross
annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 o, -o.e. 
-
(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses
or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount
of $2,500 or more.
(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the
entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more."
Thus, assuming SAG's total contributions received during 2009 remain at $100,000 or
less, Regulation 18705.3(bX2XF) will control the materiality threshold. If, however, SAG,s
contributions exceed $100,000 in 2009 but do not exceed $i,000,000, thenRegulation
18705.3(b)(2)(E), will apply, which stares as follows:
"(E) For an entity whose gross annual receipts are more than $100,000 but
less than or equal to $1,000,000 the effect of the decision will be any of
the following:
(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's gross
annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 or more.
(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses
or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount
of $12,500 or more.


Our Page 8
(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the
entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $50,000 or more."
Personal Fincmcial Effect. As to the materiality threshold for the personal financial
effect of the City Council's decisions on the Mayor, Regulation 18705.5(;) provides, in pertinent
part, as follows:
" (a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's or his
or her immediate family's personal finances is material if it is at least $250
in any 12-month period."
Therefore, if the effect of the City Council's decisions on the Mayor's personal finances is $250
or more in any l2-month period, they will be material for purposes of this analysis.
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decisions
on the Mayor's economic interest will meet that applicable materiality standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Wherher the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made
depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. (In re Thorner, supra.) A financial
effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an
effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
Thus' we have to determine whether it is substantially likely that actions of the City
Council affecting whether or not the corrective measure will be placed on the ballot will have
any of the following effects:
(1) Increasing or decreasing the amount in contributions made to SAG in a fiscal year, or
the value of SAG's assets or liabilities, by $10,000 or more (or by $50,000 or more if total
contributions to SAG in2009 are more than $100,000 and up to $1,000,000).
(2) Causing SAG to incur or avoid additional expenses or reduce or eliminate existing
expenses in a fiscal year of $10,000 or more (or $50,000 or more if total contributions to SAb in
2OO9 are more than $100,000 and up ro 91,000,000).
(3) Resulting in the personal expenses. income, assets, or liabilities of the Mayor
increasing or decreasing by $250 or more in any l2-month period.
In assessing this issue, we think it is of great significance that Mr. Hiltachk, the Mayor,s
attorney and treasurer for SAG, indicates that even if the City Council does not vote to plaCe the
corrective measure on the ballot. neither he, as the proponent of "The Strong Mayor Iniiiative,"
nor SAG intend to take any action to draft or circulate a petition to place its;wn corrective


-
Our Page 9
measllre on the ballot. In addition, Mr. Hiltachk states that he sees no legal problems with "The
Strong Mayor lnitiative" and thus sees no reason to place any corrective measure on the ballot.
Based on these facts, it is reasonable to assume that SAG would not be involved at all in
issues relating to the corrective measure and any campaign contributions it raises or debts it
incurs will relate solely to the two measures it was primarily formed to support: "The Strong
Mayor lnitiative" and the measure to create the City Office of the Independent Budget Analyst.
If this is the case, we fail to see any significant connection between the City Council's decisions
on placing the corrective measure on the ballot and a financial impact on SAG or on SAG's
ability to repay a loan to the Mayor. In addition, we think it would be highly speculative to
assert, and thus not substantially likely, that the existence of the corrective measure on the ballot
might affect, either positively or negatively, SAG's ability to raise contributions or otherwise
affect SAG's debts or liabilities. Accordingly, we do not think it is reasonably foreseeable that
the City Council's decisions affecting placement of a corrective measure on the ballot will have a
material financial effect on SAG or on the Mayor's personal finances.
Thus, we conclude that, based on the facts provided, the Mayor would not have a conflict
of interest because it is not reasonably foreseeable that council decisions related to placing the
corrective measure on the ballot will have a material financial effect on SAG or the Mayor.
Steps 7 & 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation" exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)), or if the official is
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). Since we have already
concluded that the Mayor does not have a conflict of interest in this instance, it is not necessary
to provide analysis of Steps 7 and 8.

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: Under the facts presented, the Mayor would not have a conflict of interest because it is
not reasonably foreseeable that city council decisions related to placing the second measure on
the ballot will have a material financial effect on the ballot measure committee or the Mayor.

Teichert City Attorney, City of Sacramento P.O. Box 1948 Sacramento, CA. 95812 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-09-207 Dear Ms. Teichert: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Sacramento City Council and the Mayor, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)² Because your request partly concerns conduct that has already taken place and partly concerns future actions of indefinite scope, we are providing informal assistance based on the position you pose rather than formal advice concerning a specific decision before the City Council. (Section 83114 and Regulation 18329(c)(4)(A) and (F).) Informal assistance does not provide _________________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² We note that, on September 1, 2009, we received a separate request for advice on the same issues from Thomas Hiltachk, attorney for City of Sacramento Mayor Kevin Johnson. We are issuing a separate advice letter to Mr. Hiltachk (see Hiltachk Advice Letter, No. A-09-213) on the issue of whether Mayor Johnson, as matters currently stand, can participate in City Council decisions on the issues discussed below. We view the essential facts provided in Mr. Hiltachk's request as supplemental to those provided in your request and, therefore, our advice herein is based on the facts in both your advice request and Mr. Hiltachk's. Our File No. I-09-207 Page 2 the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice (see Section 83114 and Regulation 18329(c)(3)). Nevertheless we offer this assistance as guidance for your city councilmembers, including the Mayor, on potential future decisions involving the issues discussed below. Finally, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of conflict-of-interest laws outside the Act such as common law conflict of interest. QUESTION Does the Mayor have a conflict of interest under the Act if the Mayor participates in future decisions before the City Council relating to placing a measure on the ballot that corrects perceived flaws in another measure already
